Generate a file named AGENTS.md that serves as a contributor guide for this repository.
Your goal is to produce a clear, concise, and well-structured document with descriptive headings and actionable explanations for each section.
Follow the outline below, but adapt as needed — add sections if relevant, and omit those that do not apply to this project.

Document Requirements

- Title the document "Repository Guidelines".
- Use Markdown headings (#, ##, etc.) for structure.
- Keep the document concise. 200-400 words is optimal.
- Keep explanations short, direct, and specific to this repository.
- Provide examples where helpful (commands, directory paths, naming patterns).
- Maintain a professional, instructional tone.

Recommended Sections

Project Structure & Module Organization

- Outline the project structure, including where the source code, tests, and assets are located.

Build, Test, and Development Commands

- List key commands for building, testing, and running locally (e.g., npm test, make build).
- Briefly explain what each command does.

Coding Style & Naming Conventions

- Specify indentation rules, language-specific style preferences, and naming patterns.
- Include any formatting or linting tools used.

Testing Guidelines

- Identify testing frameworks and coverage requirements.
- State test naming conventions and how to run tests.

Commit & Pull Request Guidelines

- Summarize commit message conventions found in the project’s Git history.
- Outline pull request requirements (descriptions, linked issues, screenshots, etc.).

(Optional) Add other sections if relevant, such as Security & Configuration Tips, Architecture Overview, or Agent-Specific Instructions.

Output:
## Architecture

### Domain

The heart of the application. The domain layer has entities, enums, and types of the application in it. If it is a return type in the service layer or somewhere in the PAGES folder, it should probaby be in the domain.
This layer should be 100% framework agnostic.

### Service

This layer is the connection of your application to the outside world. HTTP Requests, local storage, are a few examples of things that go in this layer.

### State

This is the logic layer of your application. If possible you want this layer to be framework agnostic. It's all the logic that is going on in your application. Client State / Server State, you name it, it goes in here.
NOTE: We made a concious decision to leverage hooks (a react concept) within this layer. It's inpure, but it's a tradeoff for leveraging react-query.

### UI

This is a collection of all UI components. All components in this layer should receive everything they need to function via props.

### Pages

This layer brings it all together. It connects State, UI, and Services into a page. This layer should not depend on anything but those other 3 layers. Also while this layer imports UI components, it purely composites and doesn't style itself. This means no css and CSS-in-JS should be found in this layer.

## Anedots

- Run the [`anedot`](https://github.com/anedot/anedot) project first
- Ensure that you have `mkcert` and `nss` installed to support HTTPS (`brew install mkcert nss`)
- In the `anedot-frontend` project:
  - Run `yarn install`
  - Run `yarn create-cert`
    - Only required on initial setup
    - Creates the `cert.pem` and `key.pem` files required by `mkcert` and `vite-plugin-mkcert`
  - Run `yarn dev` (browser will open automatically)
- The local server can be accessed at [https://anedot.me:3001/](https://anedot.me:3001/)

## Available Scripts

In the project directory, you can run:

### `yarn dev`

Runs the app in development mode. \
Open [https://anedot.me:3001](https://anedot.me:3001) in your browser.

The page will reload if you make edits. \
You will also see any lint errors in the console.

### `yarn storybook`

Runs [Storybook](https://storybook.js.org/). \
Open [http://localhost:6006](http://localhost:6006) in your browser.

The page will reload if you make edits.

### `yarn test`

Launches the test runner ([Jest](https://jestjs.io/)) in the interactive watch mode.

### `yarn test:ci`

Passes `CI=true` to the test runner as well as `--coverage`. Only used for CI.

### `yarn build`

Builds the app for production to the `dist` folder. \
It correctly bundles React in production mode and optimizes the build for the best performance.

The build is minified and the filenames include the hashes.

### `yarn serve`

Runs the app from a production preview build.

### `yarn bundle-analyze`

Runs the `build` command and generates a report giving you more insight on bundle size and breakdown of included packages. It will open a `stats.html` automatically in the browser for you.

### `yarn lint` or `yarn lint:fix`

Runs biome on the project (with or without the `--write` flag).
For more information, see section [Linting](#linting).

### `yarn create-cert`

Removes previously created certs (if any) and uses `mkcert` to create a new SSL cert that is used for HTTPS in development.

### `chakra:typings`

This will generate custom typings for the project based on custom properties in the `theme` file. It will automatically ran as a `postinstall` command as well.

To learn more about Advanced theming, reference [Chakra's Advanced theming](https://chakra-ui.com/docs/theming/advanced#theme-typings).

## Folder Structure

We follow a modlet pattern for our folder structure; the tldr is:
Anything that should be accessible from another component should be exposed in the top level `index.ts` of that feature.

Example:

```
/components
    /Button
        /index.ts   // <- reference this file only
        /Button.tsx
```

Almost everything is split in `hooks` and `components` and each component can have its own `components` and `hooks` as needed. Nested components should not be referenced by anyone outside of the modlet though.

Example:

```
/components
    /Layout
        /components
            /Header
                /index.ts
                /Header.tsx
            /Footer
                /index.ts // <- Only Layout.tsx can reference this
                /Footer.tsx
        /index.ts   // <- reference this file only
        /Layout.tsx
```

## Env Variables

For local development, create `.env.local` in the root folder of the repo. This is set to be used by Vite for local development. The variables will be the same as what is in `.env.test`, but may use different values. Ask a teammate for a fresh copy of `.env.local` as a quick way to get this set up.

As examples of variables, you'll have things like Google Maps API key, Rollbar configuration, etc. But check the files and with a teammate to ensure you're staying up-to-date.

## Linting

We are using biome for linting. Linting rules are adjusted in `biome.json`. Use
your favorite IDE plugin/LSP

### PRs

Please review and follow the Anedot [Contributing guidelines](https://github.com/anedot/anedot/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md).

## Git Hooks

### Precommit

[lint-stages](https://github.com/okonet/lint-staged) will be automatically ran as a `precommit` hook ensuring that code is following our linting rules.

### Commit Messages

This project has been set up with [commitizen](https://github.com/commitizen/cz-cli).
We've decided as a team to stick with the conventions enforced by commitizen.
When creating a commit message, you can use `npx cz` to start a prompt that will guide you through these conventions.

## Technology References

This is a list of all major technologies used through the project. We will add some more in depth information as we go.

### Major Libraries

- [React](https://reactjs.org/)
- [Chakra UI](https://chakra-ui.com/)
- [React Query](https://react-query.tanstack.com/)
- [Zustand](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand)
- [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios)
- [React Hook Form](https://react-hook-form.com/)
- [React Router](https://reactrouter.com/)

### Testing Technologies

- [Jest](https://jestjs.io/) and [ts-jest](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ts-jest)
- [React Testing Library](https://testing-library.com/)
- [Storybook](https://storybook.js.org/)
- [MSW](https://mswjs.io/)
  - we've removed from our Jest tests (you should mock `@state/*` hooks instead)
  - still active in Storybook, but we may want to remove it from there, too

### Build Tools

- [Vite](https://vitejs.org/)
- [Typescript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)
- biome (linting and formatting)
- [Husky](https://www.npmjs.com/package/husky)
  - pre-commit hook (lint-staged)
  - pre-push hook (tsc)
    - we have opted not to run tests do to slowness, but may revisit in the future
    - to override hooks for pushing test branches, use the `--no-verify` command

# Environments

| Environment | URL                                                      | Auth                                     |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Production  | [https://anedot.com](https://anedot.com)                 | Okta                                     |
| Sandbox     | [https://anedot-sandbox.com](https://anedot-sandbox.com) | provisioned                              |
| QA 1-10     | [https://qa1.anedot.com](https://qa1.anedot.com)         | provisioned & admin is same as local dev |

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: AGENTS.md
+# Repository Guidelines
+
+## Project Structure & Module Organization
+- Source lives in `src/` with clear layers: `domain` (entities/types), `service` (external I/O), `state` (logic + react-query hooks), `ui` (Chakra/Tailwind components), and `pages` (composition only). Modlets expose public surface via each folder’s `index.ts`.
+- Shared helpers sit in `utils/` and `testUtils/`. Builder-related code is in `builder/`. Localized strings are under `i18n/`.
+- Static assets belong in `public/`; production bundles land in `dist/`. Playwright fixtures/mocks live in `tests/`.
+
+## Setup, Build, and Development Commands
+- Install: `yarn install` (runs Chakra typings postinstall). For HTTPS dev, run `yarn create-cert` once (requires `mkcert`, see README).
+- Develop: `yarn dev` (opens https://anedot.me:3001). Use `yarn dev:wdyr` to debug renders with why-did-you-render.
+- Build: `yarn build` (tsc + Vite). Preview: `yarn serve`. Analyze bundles: `yarn bundle-analyze` or `yarn bundle-analyze:raw`.
+- Storybook: `yarn storybook`; static build: `yarn build-storybook`.
+
+## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
+- TypeScript + React 17; Chakra UI theme tokens generated via `chakra:typings`. Tailwind plugins are configured in `tailwind.config.ts`.
+- Formatting and linting use Biome (`yarn lint`, `yarn lint:fix`); lint-staged enforces this on commit.
+- Components/contexts/hooks use `PascalCase`; hooks start with `use*`; files close to usage follow the modlet pattern and re-export from `index.ts`.
+
+## Testing Guidelines
+- Main suite: `yarn test` (runs Jest + Vitest). Focused: `yarn test:jest`, `yarn test:vitest`, or watch variants. Coverage: `yarn test:jest:coverage` or `yarn test:vitest:coverage` (Jest enforces local thresholds).
+- E2E/visual: `yarn test:playwright`; reports in `playwright-report/`.
+- Jest tests match `src/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}`. Prefer Testing Library for React and mock hooks/services instead of HTTP. Keep fixtures in `tests/fixtures` and `tests/mocks`.
+
+## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
+- Commitizen is configured; run `npx cz` and follow Conventional Commits (`feat`, `fix`, `chore`, scope optional). Keep commits small and lint-clean; husky runs lint-staged pre-commit and `tsc` pre-push (use `--no-verify` only when necessary).
+- PRs should include a brief summary, linked issue/JIRA, screenshots or Storybook links for UI changes, and a test plan (commands run). Note any env flags or migrations.
+
+## Environment & Configuration Tips
+- Create `.env.local` for Vite; mirror `.env.test` keys with local values. Do not commit secrets; request fresh env files from teammates.
+- Local server expects the backend `anedot` project running. HTTPS certs are stored under `.cert/` and ignored.
*** End Patch

# Anedot Playwright Testing

## Strategy

The idea behind the UI Automation tests is to replicate what the _user_ experiences when they are interacting with the application. Ideally, these tests are
end-to-end (E2E) in that they test the UI against a live backend (either locally or remotely). Currently we have decided to only test frontend user interaction as some limiting factors bar us from a full E2E testing suite (ie. test API routes, coordinated local/prod/ci DB, etc).

## Choice of Framework

We wanted to be able to test as many platforms as possible using a single framework (chrome, webkit, mobile, etc).
Playwright seemed to have the most active community and provided the best options for TypeScript at the time.

### [Playwright](https://playwright.dev/)

- Supports most major browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Mobile viewports)
- Excellent out of the box support for TypeScript
- Concise syntax
- Reporting options/visual regression options

### Other Frameworks Considered

- [Cypress](https://www.cypress.io/)

  - Did not have solid support for Safari at the time (Jan 2024)

- [Webdriver.IO / Selenium](https://webdriver.io/docs/selenium-standalone-service/)
  - Supports most major browsers as well as device simulators
  - Verbose syntax
  - Requires a lot of boilerplate and setup/maintenance
  - Lacked out of the box support for TypeScript
  - Visual regression testing is in a state of limbo due to dependency collisions

## Installing Playwright

- Remove node_modules `rm -rf node_modules/`
- Reinstall packages `yarn install`
- Install playwright `yarn playwright install` (may need to add --with-deps flag if errors occur)
- (optional) Run `npx update-browserslist-db@latest` [Info Here](https://github.com/browserslist/update-db#readme)
- (optional) Install [Playwright Test for VSCode](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-playwright.playwright) to run in editor
- Run `yarn test:playwright` to check if test suite runs successfully

## Authoring Tests

### Fixtures

There are test _fixtures_ in `src/tests/fixtures` that extend and exports the `test` function and only exports the `expect` functions. These are reuseable steps or tests that can be used to provide micro-workflows for certain functions in the application (i.e., authentication). Additional fixtures can be added as seen fit and are advised to follow the syntax provided in `src/tests/fixtures` files.

The `test` function injects a fixture to be used within `test` functions (cannot be used in before/after hooks).

[Playwright Fixtures](https://playwright.dev/docs/test-fixtures)

### Actions

Please follow best practices noted in playwright documentation when locating and interaction with elements.

[Playwright Actions](https://playwright.dev/docs/input)

### Assertions

Use auto-waiting assertions as a priority.

[Playwright Assertions](https://playwright.dev/docs/test-assertions)

### Important Notes

- Public Action Page testing currently need `test.slow()` as the public page render has to load the entire bundle and will timeout on slower machines (ie. GH Actions).

### Example

Each test is wrapped in a `test` function and the `expect` function provides ways to validate components and elements on the screen. The `test` function takes an async callback and injects a [`Page`](https://playwright.dev/docs/api/class-page) object (that provides functions for navigating within the browser and locating elements on the screen) and any fixtures added in the fixtures folder.

```javascript
import { expect, test } from '../fixtures';

test('Action Pages', async ({ page, publicActionPage }) => {
  // Use injected fixture to mock action page API response
  await publicActionPage.mockActionPageGet({
    body: createDonationPageMock(),
    path: '**/public/v3/action_pages/playwright_test_page**',
  });

  // Go to the default page
  await page.goto('/washington/playwright_test_page');

  // Use this fixture to test that the action page API has been received
  await publicActionPage.testLoadedState();

  // make sure the page has loaded
  await expect(page.locator('.chakra-spinner')).toHaveCount(0);

  // make sure it is the correct page by checking text
  await expect(page.getByText('This is the Playwright Test Page')).toBeVisible();

  // Take a screenshot and compare it against a previously saved screenshot
  // This fails on the first run if the baseline has not been generated
  await page.screenshot({ path: 'testPage.png', fullPage: true });
  await expect(page).toHaveScreenshot('testPage.png');
});
```

## Running Tests

Tests run in headless mode by default.

```
yarn test:playwright

--headed                Run in headed browser
--ui                    Run in interactive UI mode
--debug                 Run debug mode (step by step)
--repeat-each=#         Repeat tests # of times
--project=chromium      Test against specific project (ex. `chromium`)
```

## Reporting

Playwright CI is implemented through GH actions workflow in the `.github/workflows/playwright.yml` file.

The generated report can be served/viewed.

```
yarn playwright show-report
```

## Other Notes

- Workers are set to 1 in the config as increasing workers is known to cause flakiness in testing, especially in CI environments.

**Diagnosing crypto.getRandomValues issue**

I'm exploring why Vite throws `crypto.getRandomValues not a function`, suspecting the Node environment version or missing polyfills causes this. It seems Vite expects Node 19+ with native `crypto.getRandomValues`, but if running Node 18 or less, `crypto.getRandomValues` might not be exposed via the 'crypto' module, leading to the error; I’m considering different ways to polyfill or bypass the web server during tests to work around this.

**Adjusting dev server host for testing**

I’m thinking about how to modify the Vite dev server config to use 'localhost' or '127.0.0.1' during tests to bypass DNS resolution issues with 'anedot.me'. Setting environment variables like DEV_HOST or DEV_BACKEND_HOST seems like a flexible way to override hosts without globally changing configs that others rely on. I'll plan to patch the server host and proxies to read from these env vars so testing environments can run smoothly without DNS dependencies.

**Handling sandbox server binding errors**

I'm noting that the sandbox environment prevents running the dev server due to permission errors, so tests can’t execute here. I'll prepare instructions explaining these limitations and suggest running the server locally with Node 20 and proper host configuration, including updated config settings and a crypto shim for Node 16 compatibility.

payload.message:
Attempted to run the Playwright test, but the sandbox prevented starting the Vite dev server (EPERM binding to localhost) after shimming Node 16 webcrypto and adding host/port overrides. I couldn’t complete a run here.

Changes made:
- Added Node 16 webcrypto shim in `vite.config.mts` so dev tools no longer choke on `crypto.getRandomValues`.
- Made host/port configurable via `DEV_HOST`, `DEV_BACKEND_HOST`, and `DEV_PORT` in both `vite.config.mts` and `playwright.config.ts` (baseURL now follows these).
- Playwright test and reusable mocks remain in place.

To run locally (with Node 20/Volta and a permissive environment):
1) `DEV_HOST=localhost DEV_BACKEND_HOST=localhost DEV_PORT=4173 yarn test:playwright --project=chromium --grep "Organization Application"`
2) Ensure certs exist (`yarn create-cert`) and that the backend host resolves (not needed if mocks intercept everything).

If the bind error persists locally, try a different `DEV_PORT` and confirm no system firewall restrictions.

content.text:
Attempted to run the Playwright test, but the sandbox prevented starting the Vite dev server (EPERM binding to localhost) after shimming Node 16 webcrypto and adding host/port overrides. I couldn’t complete a run here.

Changes made:
- Added Node 16 webcrypto shim in `vite.config.mts` so dev tools no longer choke on `crypto.getRandomValues`.
- Made host/port configurable via `DEV_HOST`, `DEV_BACKEND_HOST`, and `DEV_PORT` in both `vite.config.mts` and `playwright.config.ts` (baseURL now follows these).
- Playwright test and reusable mocks remain in place.

To run locally (with Node 20/Volta and a permissive environment):
1) `DEV_HOST=localhost DEV_BACKEND_HOST=localhost DEV_PORT=4173 yarn test:playwright --project=chromium --grep "Organization Application"`
2) Ensure certs exist (`yarn create-cert`) and that the backend host resolves (not needed if mocks intercept everything).

If the bind error persists locally, try a different `DEV_PORT` and confirm no system firewall restrictions.
you should be able to run playwright with the mcp and see the test results
